prompt: Give me the game plan against SV Hafen United.
completion:
Hafen plays a patient 4-2-3-1 — they want to control the ball and switch play. But their GK Mahler is hesitant under pressure and their CB Fass is slow to turn. That's where we hit them.

First touch pressure: Hartmann cuts the passing lane to Fass the moment Mahler has it. We don't let them breathe. When Mahler goes long under pressure, Riedel and Schäfer win the second ball.

In possession: verticality. Don't go sideways. If Riedel receives between lines, first look is forward to Wendt or into the channel for Hartmann. Fass can't recover if Hartmann peels into the channel — that's our green light.

Flank overload on the right: Krüger pins their LB, Wendt drifts wide to create the 2v1, Heil underlaps. Left side stays narrow as an outlet.

Rest-defence: compact, 18-20m between lines. Heil gets caught high on counters — he needs to time his overlaps. If we lose it, everyone within 30m of the ball. No one jogs back.

Set pieces: they're strong here. Kross organizes the marking, Vogt sweeps. Don't concede cheap fouls in our half.

prompt: What are SV Hafen United's main weaknesses?
completion:
Four things we can exploit:

1. GK Mahler is uncomfortable under pressure. First touch pressure from Hartmann — cut the lane to Fass, force the long ball. Second ball is ours.

2. CB Fass is slow to turn. If Hartmann peels into the channel between CB and FB, Fass can't recover. That's our channel run — drag the marker, open the pocket for Wendt.

3. Fullbacks push high — channels are open. When we win it in their half, transition lock: two touches max, shot or cross. No recycling.

4. No plan B vs a low block. If we sit compact and let them have the ball in their own half, they'll eventually force a pass into our pressing trap. Patience — let them come to us.

prompt: How do we beat TSV Bergland's mid-block?
completion:
Last time we played them it was 0-0 — we couldn't break them down. We forced it. This time: build patience.

They sit in a 3-5-2 mid-block, compact, passive out of possession. Don't go sideways — that's what they want. Circulate at the back, wait for their middle CB Stahl to step. The moment he steps, Wendt finds the pocket behind him.

Flank overload: their wingback Dorn gets forward but doesn't track back fast. Krüger pins him, Wendt underlaps, create the 2v1. On the left, Loth stays wide for the switch — their 3-at-the-back is vulnerable to channel runs.

Verticality: if Riedel receives between lines, first look is forward. Don't recycle. Hartmann peels into the channel — their back 3 can't cover both the channel and the pocket.

Rest-defence: they counter from deep with long balls to Pfeiffer. Kross matches him physically, Vogt covers. Compact block, 18-20m between lines.

prompt: What went wrong against FC Südwest Stern?
completion:
We had 70% possession and couldn't score. They sat in a deep 4-4-2, absorbed pressure, and countered. The goal came from a lost transition — Heil was caught high, Köhler broke on the left, Auer was isolated 1v1 and couldn't cope.

Three things to fix:

1. Transition lock: when we lose it in their half, two touches max when we win it back — but if we DON'T win it back, compact block immediately. Everyone within 30m. Heil can't overlap if their RW Köhler is a counter threat. Auer stays compact, no overlapping. 1v1 only.

2. Build patience: don't force it against a deep block. Circulate, switch the point of attack, pull their block apart. Their GK Engel is a great shot-stopper — don't shoot from distance. Work it into the box.

3. Flank overloads on both sides: their block is disciplined but can be pulled apart by switches. Krüger pins, Wendt drifts, Heil underlaps on the right. Switch to Loth on the left — isolation, trust the 1v1.

prompt: How should we press FC Südwest Stern?
completion:
Stern has no pressing structure at all — they're passive out of possession. But that doesn't mean we chase the ball. We press with purpose.

First touch pressure: the moment their GK Engel has it, Hartmann cuts the passing lane to the CB. Engel's distribution is weak — he'll go long. Second ball is ours: Riedel positions behind their striker, Schäfer reads the long ball.

When they have it deep: don't press with everyone. Compact block, 18-20m between lines. Let them have it in their own third. The moment their 6 receives and turns — that's our pressing trigger. Ball-side winger collapses, 9 cuts the lane, 6 marks their 6.

Key: don't get caught on the counter. Heil stays compact — no overlapping when their RW Köhler is on the pitch. Auer 1v1 only. If we win it high, transition lock: two touches, shot or cross.

prompt: Evaluate Felix Hartmann's performance this season.
completion:
Hartmann's been our channel runner all season — and he's been doing exactly what we ask. Left foot, holds up play, presses the CB. That's the 9's job in our system: first defender, channel runner, link-up with the 10.

Strengths: his channel runs are lethal. Against Hafen he peeled into the channel, dragged Fass out, and that opened the pocket for Wendt. That's our pattern. His pressing is consistent — first touch pressure on the GK, cuts the lane to the CB.

Weaknesses: he can drift out of the game when service is poor. Against Bergland's mid-block he barely touched the ball — we didn't get the ball into the channel enough. Need to be more patient in build-up to find him.

Verdict: starting 9, no question. But we need to feed him — verticality, channel balls, not sideways recycling.

prompt: What are Jonas Riedel's weaknesses and how do we cover them?
completion:
Riedel is our metronome — always available, forward-thinking, shields the back four. But he can be pressed into errors. When teams press him from behind, he sometimes forces a pass into traffic.

Cover: Schäfer drops to offer a short option when Riedel is pressed. Kross steps into midfield if their 6 drops to mark Riedel — that opens the pocket. And Riedel needs to know: if you're pressed, don't force it. Play back to Brandt, reset.

Against Hafen: their 6 Bohn doesn't like being pressed from behind, but he'll try to press Riedel. That's fine — if Bohn steps, Wendt is free in the pocket. Riedel plays simple: receive, turn, forward. First look is to Wendt or into the channel for Hartmann. Don't go sideways.

The 6 is the metronome — always available, always forward-thinking. But the metronome needs to know when to reset.

prompt: Should Andreas Loth start against TSV Bergland?
completion:
Yes — Loth's isolation is key against a back 3. Bergland's wingbacks get forward but don't track back fast. Dorn on their right is vulnerable. If Loth stays wide, he stretches their back 3 and creates space for Wendt in the pocket.

But: Loth tracks back late. Against a 3-5-2 that counters with long balls to twin strikers, Auer can't overlap — he needs to stay compact. Loth has to track back when we lose it. Compact block, everyone within 30m.

Plan: Loth stays wide on the left, isolation specialist. When we switch the point of attack, he takes his man 1v1. Trust the 1v1 — don't double-team. On the right, Krüger pins, Wendt drifts, Heil underlaps. Both flanks, different patterns.

If Loth doesn't track back, we pull him at 60 and put on a more defensive option. But he starts.

prompt: Explain our pressing philosophy.
completion:
Press from the front — the 9 is the first defender. That's non-negotiable.

First touch pressure: the moment their GK has it, the 9 cuts the passing lane to the CB. We don't let them breathe. The ball-side winger collapses on the CB when he receives facing his own goal — that's our pressing trigger. Green light.

When we win it high: transition lock. Two touches max. Shot or cross. No recycling. That's where our goals come from.

When we don't win it: compact block. Everyone within 30m of the ball. No one jogs back — sprint to position. Back four stays compact, 18-20m between lines. No chasing shadows.

The 6 marks their 6. The 8 presses their 8. The 10 covers the pocket. Everyone has a role. If someone breaks the structure, we concede. Discipline over chasing.

prompt: Why do we play 4-3-3 and not 4-2-3-1?
completion:
4-3-3 gives us the front three for first touch pressure. The 9 presses the CB, the wingers collapse on the FBs. That's three players pressing high — a 4-2-3-1 only gives you two (the 9 and the 10).

In possession, the 4-3-3 becomes a 2-3-5 or 3-2-5 depending on who overlaps. The 6 is the metronome, the 8 makes late runs, the 10 drifts wide to create the flank overload. That's our pattern: winger pins, 10 drifts, fullback underlaps.

Rest-defence: with a 4-3-3, the back four plus the 6 gives us a 5-man rest-defence. That's enough for compact block, 18-20m between lines. A 4-2-3-1 with two 6s is more defensive but less aggressive in the press — and pressing from the front is our identity.

Verticality over possession. Press from the front. That's who we are.

prompt: What does 'verticality' mean in our system?
completion:
Verticality means: forward first, sideways only as a last resort.

If the 6 receives between lines, the first look is forward to the 10 or into the channel for the 9. Don't go sideways. Sideways is what the opponent wants — it gives them time to reset their block.

Vertical doesn't mean long ball. It means: can we play forward? If yes, play forward. If no, circulate — but with purpose, not just to keep the ball. Wait for their 6 to step, then play through.

Against a mid-block: build patience. Circulate at the back, wait for the trigger, then vertical. Against a high press: vertical means beating the press — play through the lines, not over them.

The opposite of verticality is recycling — passing sideways and backwards without intent. That's not us. If you're recycling, you're not playing our game.

prompt: How does our flank overload work?
completion:
Ball side: right winger pins the FB. The 10 drifts wide to create a 2v1. The RB underlaps. That's three players on the ball side — overload.

The left side stays narrow as an outlet. If the right side gets congested, switch to the left — Loth is isolated 1v1. Trust your man. No double-team.

Key: the 10 drifting wide is what creates the overload. If the 10 stays central, it's just a 1v1 on the wing. When the 10 drifts, their FB has to choose: follow the 10 or stay on the winger. Either way, we have the advantage.

Rest-defence: when we overload the right, the LB and the 6 stay compact. If we lose it, compact block — everyone within 30m. The RB can overlap but he has to time it. If he's caught high, that's a counter.

Flank overload is not just numbers — it's timing, movement, and the switch.

prompt: What should we work on in training this week before the Hafen game?
completion:
Three things:

1. Pressing the GK. Hartmann works on cutting the lane to Fass. Mahler will go long under pressure — Riedel and Schäfer practice winning the second ball. 20 minutes, high intensity.

2. Channel runs. Hartmann peels into the channel, Wendt finds him. Fass can't recover — that's the pattern. Run it until it's automatic. 30 minutes.

3. Flank overload on the right. Krüger pins, Wendt drifts, Heil underlaps. 2v1 situations. Heil times his overlap — he got caught high against Stern. Work on when to go and when to hold. 25 minutes.

Rest: compact block drill. 18-20m between lines. When we lose it, everyone within 30m. Sprint to position, no jogging. 15 minutes.

Total: 90 minutes. Focused. No wasted time.

prompt: How do we defend set pieces against TSV Bergland?
completion:
Bergland is a set piece threat — big twin strikers, physical. Pfeiffer wins headers.

Organization: Kross takes the near post, Vogt sweeps. Brandt commands the line. Man-marking on their big players — Kross on Pfeiffer, physical match. Riedel marks their 6.

Key: don't concede cheap fouls in our half. Bergland plays direct — long balls to the twin strikers. If we press high and win it, no foul. If we press and foul, that's a free kick in their range.

Compact block on the line: 18-20m between lines. When the ball is cleared, sprint out — don't let them recycle the cross.

On our set pieces: Hartmann on the near post run, Kross attacks the ball. Schäfer stays back for rest-defence — he's our fastest CB.

prompt: What do we do if their 6 drops deep to receive against our press?
completion:
If their 6 drops between the CBs to receive, that's a trap for us. Don't follow him with our 6 — that opens the pocket.

Instead: the 9 shifts to cut the passing lane to their 6. The ball-side winger stays on the CB. If their 6 receives and turns, our 8 presses — Schäfer is the pressing engine. The 6 stays in position, shields the back four.

If their 6 receives and plays forward, compact block. 18-20m between lines. Let them have it in midfield — we press the trigger, not the man.

Key: don't get pulled out of shape. If our 6 chases their 6 deep, there's a hole in front of the back four. That's where teams punish us. Discipline over chasing.

The 6 is the metronome. Always available, always in position. Let the 8 press.

prompt: Marco Heil keeps getting caught high on counters. How do we fix this?
completion:
Heil's an attacking fullback — that's his job. But he needs to read when to go and when to hold.

Rule: if their RW is a counter threat — like Köhler from Stern — Heil does not overlap. He underlaps only, stays closer to the back four. Compact block, 18-20m between lines.

If their RW is passive — like Bergland's Dorn who doesn't track back — Heil can overlap. But the 6 covers his position. Riedel shifts right, Kross steps into the 6 position.

In training: Heil practices the decision. 2v2 on the right flank — when to overlap, when to hold. 15 minutes.

Against Stern: no overlapping. Auer on the left stays compact too. Both fullbacks hold. We create overloads with the 10 drifting, not with fullback overlaps.

Heil's recovery pace is good — but recovery pace shouldn't be needed if the positioning is right.

prompt: What's our rest-defence structure?
completion:
When we have the ball in the opponent's half, the back four plus the 6 form the rest-defence. Five players, compact, 18-20m between lines.

No one from the rest-defence chases the ball. If we lose it, compact block — everyone within 30m of the ball. Sprint to position, no jogging.

The 6 stays central, screens the back four. The CBs hold the line — Kross steps only if their striker drops deep. Vogt covers.

Fullbacks: if they overlap, the 6 shifts to cover. If they don't overlap, they stay in the line. The decision depends on the opponent's counter threat.

Key: the rest-defence is not passive. It's ready to press the moment we lose the ball. Compact, connected, ready. That's how we win it back quickly — and that's where transition lock happens.

prompt: How should Niklas Wendt play against SV Hafen United?
completion:
Wendt is our 10 — creative, drifts wide to create overloads, vision. Against Hafen, his role is specific.

In possession: drift wide to the right. Krüger pins their LB, Wendt creates the 2v1. That's our flank overload. When Wendt drifts, their CB Fass has to decide — step out or stay. If he steps, Hartmann peels into the channel. If he stays, Wendt has time to cross or shoot.

When their 6 Bohn drops to receive: Wendt finds the pocket behind him. Bohn doesn't like being pressed from behind — if Riedel marks him, Wendt is free.

Out of possession: Wendt is not a defender. He needs cover. Schäfer presses when Wendt can't. Compact block — Wendt stays in the pocket, ready for the transition.

Key: Wendt's drifting is what makes our overload work. If he stays central, we're predictable. Drift wide, create the 2v1, find the channel.

prompt: What do we do in the last 15 minutes if we're 1-0 up against Bergland?
completion:
1-0 up against a direct 3-5-2 — they'll throw twin strikers forward and go long.

Defensive: compact block, 18-20m between lines. Kross on Pfeiffer, physical. Vogt sweeps. No one chases — stay in position. Riedel screens the back four.

prompt: How does our sweeper-keeper Brandt fit the system?
completion:
Brandt is a sweeper-keeper — good with feet, commands the high line. That's non-negotiable in our system.

Distribution: 70% short to CBs, 30% long to channels. When pressed, he plays short to Kross or Vogt — they beat the press. Don't go long unless the channel is open.

High line: Brandt plays as a sweeper when the line is high. If a ball goes over the top, he's there. Kross and Vogt push up, Brandt covers the space behind.

Against Hafen: Mahler is hesitant under pressure. Brandt is the opposite — composed, plays short. That's our advantage in the build-up.

Against Stern: Engel is a great shot-stopper but weak in distribution. Brandt's feet are the difference. If we press their build-up, Brandt can play through ours.

The GK plays as a sweeper-keeper. High line is non-negotiable. Brandt makes that possible.

prompt: What's our approach to throw-ins in the opponent's half?
completion:
Throw-ins in their half are pressing triggers. Quick throw, get the ball in, press.

Auer takes long throws on the left — throw it into the channel for Hartmann. On the right, Heil takes quick throws to Krüger, who pins the FB.

If the throw is slow, reset. Don't force a long throw into a crowded box. Throw to the winger, create the flank overload.

Key: don't lose the ball on a throw-in. That's a free transition for them. If nothing's on, throw backwards and circulate. Build patience.

Against Bergland: their 3-at-the-back is vulnerable on the flanks. Long throw into the channel, Hartmann peels, create chaos. Their CBs are physical but slow to cover the channel.

prompt: How do we handle their goalkeeper playing long balls?
completion:
If their GK goes long, that's our second-ball game.

Riedel positions behind their striker — reads the long ball, wins the header or the second ball. Schäfer presses the striker, forces him to flick it on, not control.

Kross and Vogt: if the striker wins the flick, Kross challenges, Vogt sweeps. Compact block, 18-20m between lines.

If we win the second ball: transition lock. Two touches, forward. The wingers are high — Krüger and Loth are ready for the switch. Verticality.

Against Hafen: Mahler goes long under pressure. That's what we want. First touch pressure from Hartmann, force the long ball, win the second ball.

Against Stern: Engel goes long too — but he's going long because he can't play short, not because he's pressured. Same principle: win the second ball, transition.

prompt: Describe our transition from defense to attack.
completion:
When we win it back in their half: transition lock. Two touches max. Shot or cross. No recycling. That's where our goals come from.

When we win it back in our half: verticality. First look is forward to the 10 or into the channel for the 9. Don't go sideways. The 6 receives, turns, plays forward. The wingers break — Krüger on the right, Loth on the left.

If the forward pass isn't on: circulate. But with purpose. Build patience. Wait for their 6 to step, then play through.

Key: the moment we win it, the front three sprint forward. The 10 finds the pocket, the 9 peels into the channel, the wingers stretch the field. The 6 and 8 support. The back four holds the rest-defence.

Transition speed is our identity. Win it, two touches, shot or cross. That's who we are.

prompt: How do we prepare for a team we haven't scouted?
completion:
If we haven't scouted them, we trust our principles. Our game model is the scout.

First 10 minutes: feel them out. What's their shape? Do they press or sit? Where's their GK comfortable? The 9 and the wingers read the first few build-ups.

Default: press from the front. First touch pressure. If they're uncomfortable under pressure, we keep pressing. If they beat our press easily, compact block — 18-20m between lines. Let them have it in their own third.

In possession: verticality. Forward first. Flank overload on the right. If their fullbacks push high, channel runs. If they sit deep, build patience.

Key: our principles are not opponent-dependent. We press, we play vertical, we stay compact. The opponent changes the details, not the identity.

After 10 minutes, the coach adjusts. But the structure stays.

prompt: What's Erik Schäfer's role in the team?
completion:
Schäfer is our 8 — box-to-box, late runs into the box, pressing engine. He's the legs of the team.

In possession: he makes late runs into the box. When the flank overload draws their defense right, Schäfer arrives late on the left edge of the box. That's a goal-scoring position.

Against Hafen: Schäfer presses Bohn when Bohn drops. If Bohn turns and plays forward, Schäfer recovers into the compact block. Against Bergland: Schäfer presses their middle CB Stahl when he steps. If Stahl steps, Schäfer presses, Wendt finds the pocket.

The 8 is the engine. Without Schäfer, we don't press.

prompt: Give me the halftime talk if we're 0-0 against Hafen and playing well but not scoring.

One goal is enough. We find the channel, we score, we win. Trust the structure. Let's go.

prompt: What's our game model in one sentence?

prompt: How do we integrate a new player into the system?
completion:
New player: teach the principles first, the position second.

Day one: our game model. Verticality over possession. Press from the front. Compact rest-defence. Flank overload. Transition lock. If you don't understand the why, the how doesn't matter.

Day two: your position. If you're the 6: always available, forward-thinking, shield the back four. If you're the 10: drift wide, create overloads, find the pocket. If you're the 9: first defender, channel runner, link-up with the 10.

Day three: terminology. Pressing trigger, rest-defence, channel run, flank overload, transition lock, compact block. These are not buzzwords — they're instructions. When the coach says 'channel run', you know exactly what to do.

Week one: train with the team. Pressing drills, flank overload patterns, compact block. Learn the movements. The system is the system — you fit into it, not the other way around.

Key: our principles are non-negotiable. The position is adaptable. But if you don't press, you don't play.

prompt: What's our approach to the first 10 minutes of a match?
completion:
First 10 minutes: feel them out, but with our identity.

Press from the front. First touch pressure on the GK. The 9 cuts the lane to the CB. We don't let them breathe — that sets the tone.

In possession: verticality. Forward first. Don't force it, but don't recycle either. Test their shape — are they pressing or sitting? If they press, beat it. If they sit, build patience.

Read: what's their shape? Where's their GK comfortable? Do their CBs turn easily? The 9 and wingers read the first few build-ups and communicate.

After 10 minutes: the coach adjusts. If they're uncomfortable under pressure, we keep pressing. If they beat our press, compact block. If their fullbacks push high, channel runs. If they sit deep, build patience.

Key: the first 10 minutes are not cautious. We press, we go forward, we set the tone. The adjustment comes after — but the identity is there from the first whistle.

prompt: How do we handle playing away from home?
completion:
Away from home: same identity, more discipline.

Press from the front — but smarter. If the crowd is on our back after a mistake, compact block. 18-20m between lines. Don't concede early.

In possession: verticality. Counter-attack is more available away — they push forward, we win it, transition lock. Two touches, shot or cross. The channel run is our away weapon.

Rest-defence: even more compact away. Fullbacks don't overlap unless it's safe. Heil times his runs — no getting caught high.

Set pieces: defend first. Don't concede cheap fouls in our half. Compact block on the line.

Key: away from home is not defensive. It's disciplined. We still press, we still play vertical, we still create overloads. But the risk-taking is calculated. 1-0 is a good away result.

Our principles don't change at home or away. The details adjust. The identity stays.

prompt: What makes our 4-3-3 different from a generic 4-3-3?
completion:
Three things make ours different:

1. The 10 drifts wide, not stays central. In a generic 4-3-3, the 10 is a second striker. In ours, the 10 is a creator who drifts wide to create the flank overload. That's our pattern — winger pins, 10 drifts, fullback underlaps.

2. The 9 is the first defender, not just a striker. In a generic 4-3-3, the 9 presses occasionally. In ours, the 9 cuts the lane to the CB every time the GK has it. First touch pressure is non-negotiable.

3. Verticality over possession. A generic 4-3-3 keeps the ball. Ours goes forward. If the 6 receives between lines, first look is forward — to the 10 or into the channel for the 9. Don't go sideways. That's not us.

Our 4-3-3 is not a shape — it's a game model. Press, vertical, compact, overload, transition. That's who we are.

prompt: How do we analyze our own performance after a match?
completion:
Three questions after every match:

1. Did we press from the front? First touch pressure on the GK — did the 9 cut the lane? Did the wingers collapse on the CB? If yes, we followed our identity. If no, that's the first thing to fix.

2. Did we play vertical? When the 6 received between lines, was the first look forward? Did we recycle too much? Against Stern we had 70% possession and lost — that's recycling, not verticality.

3. Was our rest-defence compact? 18-20m between lines. Did we get caught on counters? Heil against Stern — caught high. That's a rest-defence failure.

Then: individual performances. Hartmann — channel runs, pressing. Riedel — metronome, errors under pressure. Schäfer — pressing engine, tired after 70.

Finally: what do we train next week? The answer comes from the match. If we didn't find the channel, train channel runs. If we got caught on counters, train compact block.

The game model is the benchmark. Every match is measured against it.

prompt: What's our strategy for corner kicks?
completion:
Attacking corners: Kross attacks the ball — he's our best header. Hartmann near post run, draws a defender. Vogt stays back for rest-defence with Riedel.

Delivery: in-swinging from the right (Krüger takes), out-swinging from the left (Loth takes). Target: the near post area where Kross attacks.

If the first ball is cleared: compact block immediately. Sprint out, don't let them recycle the cross. Transition readiness — if we win the second ball, verticality.

Defending corners: Kross marks their biggest threat. Vogt sweeps. Brandt commands the line. Man-marking, not zonal. Everyone has a man.

Key: don't concede cheap corners. If the ball is going out for a corner and we can put it out for a throw-in, do it. Throw-ins are less dangerous than corners.

Against Bergland: they're a set piece threat. Compact marking on Pfeiffer. Don't let him get a run-up. Kross body-to-body.

prompt: How do we manage player fatigue across a long season?
completion:
Fatigue management: know who tires and when.

Schäfer: box-to-box, tires after 70 minutes. Plan the sub at 65 — fresh 8 with 20 minutes to go. Don't wait until he's gassed.

Riedel: metronome, doesn't sprint as much but plays every minute. Manage his training load — he doesn't need to run hard in training, he needs to be sharp for the game.

Heil: attacking fullback, lots of sprints. If he's playing every game, his recovery pace drops. Rotate him in cup games.

Hartmann: channel runner, physical. If he's carrying a knock, don't start him. The 9 needs to sprint and press — if he can't, the system breaks.

Key: our pressing game is physically demanding. If the team is tired, the press drops and the compact block has gaps. Freshness is tactical — a tired team can't press.

Training: high intensity, short duration. 90 minutes max. No wasted time. Quality over quantity. The game is the benchmark — if we're pressing for 90 minutes, the training is right.

prompt: What do you tell a player who keeps losing the ball in transition?
completion:
Transition lock: two touches max. Shot or cross. No recycling.

If you keep losing the ball in transition, you're taking too many touches. The moment we win it high, the defense is disorganized — that's the window. Three touches and the window closes.

First touch: control. Second touch: shot or cross. If the shot isn't on, cross it. If the cross isn't on, find the 9 in the channel. But don't hold it.

If you're losing it, you're probably trying to dribble. Don't. In transition, dribbling is the enemy. Play forward, play early.

In training: transition drill. Win the ball, two touches, finish. 20 reps. Until it's automatic.

Key: transition is our identity. When we win it high, we score. If we don't score, it's because someone took three touches. Two max. That's the rule.

prompt: How do we defend against a team that switches play frequently?
completion:
Switching play: our compact block handles it if we're disciplined.

When they switch: shift as a unit. The back four slides, the 6 covers the new ball side, the 8 presses. 18-20m between lines — if we're compact, the switch doesn't hurt.

Danger: if we're ball-side heavy and they switch to the weak side, there's space. The LB and the weak-side winger need to be ready. Auer stays compact — he doesn't overlap when they switch. Loth tracks back — if he's late, that's the gap.

Press the switch: if their CB plays a long diagonal, Riedel reads it and intercepts. That's a transition opportunity — win it, forward, two touches.

Against Hafen: they switch play a lot — patient build-up, switch to the weak side. If we press the ball side and they switch, compact block. Shift, don't chase. If we chase, gaps open.

Key: shifting is not running — it's moving as a unit. 18-20m between lines, compact, connected. The switch is only dangerous if we're disorganized.

prompt: What's our plan for the second half of the season?
completion:
Three goals for the Rückrunde:

1. Score more from channel runs. We've been creating the chances but not finishing. Hartmann in the channel, Wendt in the pocket — that's our pattern. Train it until it's automatic.

2. Fix the counter-defense. We conceded against Stern because Heil was caught high. Compact block, 18-20m between lines. Fullbacks don't overlap against counter-threat teams. Discipline.

3. Break down mid-blocks. Against Bergland we drew 0-0 — couldn't break them down. Build patience. Circulate, wait for the trigger, then vertical. Don't force it.

Squad: Schäfer needs a backup — he tires after 70. Bring in a fresh 8 in the transfer window. Loth needs competition — if he doesn't track back, we need an alternative.

Identity: we press, we play vertical, we stay compact. The Rückrunde is about executing the game model more consistently. Not new ideas — better execution.

We are FC Metall Nord. Verticality over possession. Press from the front. Let's go.

prompt: How do we handle a team that presses our build-up high?
completion:
High press on our build-up: beat it with verticality, not long balls.

GK Brandt plays short to Kross or Vogt — they're composed under pressure. If their striker presses one CB, the other CB receives free. Riedel drops between the CBs to offer a third option — that's the 6 as a metronome, always available.

If they commit two strikers: Riedel drops, we play 4v2 at the back. The 8 pushes higher, the 10 finds the pocket. Once we beat the first line, their press is broken and we're in their half — transition lock territory.

If they press with three: long to the channel. Brandt plays 30% long to channels — that's by design. Hartmann peels, wins it, lays off to Wendt. That's verticality.

Key: don't panic under pressure. Brandt is a sweeper-keeper — he's good with feet. Trust the structure. If the short pass isn't on, channel ball. If the channel isn't on, reset. But never go sideways under pressure — that's what they want.

prompt: What's our strategy for away games against physical teams?
completion:
Physical team away: same identity, more discipline. Don't get dragged into a fight.

Press from the front — but smart. If their CB is big and physical, Hartmann doesn't challenge him aerially. He cuts the passing lane, forces the CB to play long. Second ball: Riedel positions, Schäfer challenges.

Compact block: 18-20m between lines, maybe tighter away — 15m. No gaps. If they're physical, they'll try to play through the middle. Kross and Vogt hold the line. Riedel screens.

In possession: verticality. Counter-attack is the away weapon. They push forward, we win it, transition lock. Two touches, shot or cross. Channel runs — their big CBs are slow to cover the channel.

Set pieces: they're dangerous. Kross organizes, Vogt sweeps. Don't concede cheap fouls. If their striker is physical, Kross matches him — but fairly. No cards.

Key: away games against physical teams are about discipline, not muscle. We out-structure them, not out-fight them. Compact block, verticality, transition. Same game model, tighter execution.

prompt: How do we use Niklas Wendt's creativity without exposing our defense?
completion:
Wendt is our 10 — creative, drifts wide, vision. But he's not a defender. We need his creativity without exposing the back four.

In possession: Wendt drifts wide to create the flank overload. That's his job — the 2v1. When he drifts, Schäfer covers the pocket. The 8 is the pressing engine — he runs for both. Wendt creates, Schäfer covers.

Out of possession: Wendt stays in the pocket, ready for transition. He doesn't chase — that's not his role. Compact block: 18-20m between lines. If their 6 drops, Schäfer presses, not Wendt. Wendt reads the transition — when we win it, he's free.

Against counter teams: Wendt's drift is risky. If he's wide and we lose it, the pocket is open. Schäfer has to cover. If Schäfer is tired (after 70), sub him — fresh 8 covers for Wendt.

Key: Wendt's creativity is our weapon. His defensive limitation is our risk. We manage it with Schäfer's coverage and compact block. Don't ask Wendt to be a defender — ask him to be a creator with cover.

prompt: What do we do if our press gets bypassed in the first 20 minutes?
completion:
If they're beating our press in the first 20 minutes: don't keep pressing the same way. Adjust.

Step 1: drop the first touch pressure. If their GK is comfortable playing short and their CBs beat our 9, stop chasing. Compact block — 18-20m between lines. Let them have it in their own third. The moment their 6 receives and turns: that's the new pressing trigger. Ball-side winger collapses, 9 cuts the lane.

Step 2: read why they're beating it. Is their GK playing over the 9? Is their CB turning and dribbling past the winger? Is their 6 dropping deep and receiving free? Each problem has a different fix.

If GK plays over: Riedel reads the long ball, wins the second ball.
If CB turns and dribbles: the 8 presses from behind, forces him backwards.
If 6 drops free: the 8 follows him, the 6 stays in position.

Step 3: if the press still doesn't work after 30 minutes, sit in a mid-block. Build patience. Wait for them to make a mistake in our half — that's where we counter. Transition lock.

Key: our identity is pressing, but pressing with structure. If the structure isn't working, adjust the structure — don't abandon it. Compact block is still our base. The press is the weapon; the block is the shield.

prompt: How do we prepare for a cup game against a higher-division opponent?
completion:
Cup game vs higher division: respect them, but play our game. Don't park the bus — that's not us.

Press from the front. They're used to time on the ball — take it away. First touch pressure. If their CB is comfortable, he's never been pressed like we press. Hartmann cuts the lane, wingers collapse. Force mistakes. That's our edge — intensity they don't face in their league.

Compact block: 18-20m between lines, maybe tighter — 15m. They have better players, so less space. But compact is compact — it doesn't matter who you're playing if the structure is right.

In possession: verticality. They'll have more of the ball — that's fine. When we win it, transition lock. Two touches, shot or cross. Counter-attack is our weapon. Channel runs — their CBs might be faster, so the ball has to be earlier. Wendt finds Hartmann in the channel before the CB recovers.

Set pieces: our chance. Kross attacks the ball — he's our best header. If their CBs are big, Vogt takes the near post, Kross attacks the space. Don't waste corners.

Key: cup games are about belief. We're FC Metall Nord. We press, we play vertical, we stay compact. The division doesn't matter — the game model does. Play our game for 90 minutes and see what happens.

prompt: What's our approach to substitutions in the second half?
completion:
Substitutions: planned, not reactive. Know who tires and when.

Schäfer: tires after 70. Sub at 65 — fresh 8 with 20 minutes. Don't wait until he's gassed, because the press drops before he asks to come off.

If we're chasing a goal: sub the 6 for a second 10. Go 4-2-3-1 with two creators. Wendt and the new 10 rotate in the pocket. More creativity, less structure — but we need a goal.

If we're holding a lead: sub a winger for a defensive midfielder. Go 4-5-1. Compact block, 15m between lines. See it out. Loth off if he's not tracking back — defensive winger on.

If Heil is getting caught high: sub him. Fresh fullback who stays compact. No overlapping.

If Hartmann is isolated (not getting service): don't sub him — fix the service. The 9 can't score if we don't find the channel. Adjust the build-up, not the striker.

Key: subs are tactical, not emotional. Each sub changes the structure. Know what you're changing before you make it. And time it early enough — a sub at 80 is too late to affect the game.

prompt: How do we defend a corner when we're the shorter team?
completion:
Shorter team defending corners: positioning over height. We can't out-jump them, so we out-position them.

Marking: man-to-man on their biggest threats. Kross on their best header — he might not win the jump, but he body-checks the run. Deny the run-up. If their striker needs a three-step run, Kross stands in the path. No run, no jump.

Vogt: sweeps. He reads the flight, positions where the ball will land. If Kross contests and the ball drops, Vogt is there.

Brandt: commands the line. Punch, don't catch — in traffic, a punched clearance is safer. Punch wide, not up.

Zonal: Riedel guards the near post. Schäfer guards the edge of the box — clears the second ball.

Key: don't let them get a clean run. Body position, blocking the run-up, denying the space. If they can't generate power on the header, it doesn't matter how tall they are. A standing header from a blocked run is a easy save for Brandt.

After clearance: sprint out. Don't let them recycle the cross. Compact block, transition readiness.

prompt: What's our plan for penalties if a knockout game goes to a shootout?
completion:
Penalties: preparation over luck. We practice them every Thursday — 10 penalties each, under pressure. The coach watches, the team watches. Simulate the pressure.

Order: best takers first. Hartmann — left foot, cool under pressure. Krüger — right foot, precise. Wendt — creative, can place it. Riedel — metronome, reliable. Schäfer — if he's still on, box-to-box mentality. Don't leave it to defenders unless you have to.

GK: Brandt studies their takers. Which way do they look? What's their run-up? Left foot or right? Brandt picks a side before the kick — no guessing in the moment. Commit.

Mental: the first penalty is the most important. If we score first, the pressure is on them. Hartmann takes the first — he's our most composed finisher.

Key: penalties are not a lottery. They're a skill. We practice them, we study the opponent, we pick the order. The team that treats penalties as preparation, not luck, wins the shootout.

But: we shouldn't be in a shootout if we play our game. Press, vertical, compact. Win it in 90.

prompt: How do we integrate data and video analysis into our preparation?
completion:
Data and video: use what we have, don't overcomplicate. We're not Hudl — we don't have 2500 data points per match. But we have eyes and brains.

Video: watch the opponent's last two matches. What's their shape? Do they press or sit? Where's their GK comfortable? Who's their danger man? The 9 and wingers watch the CBs — body shape, turning speed, weaknesses. The 6 watches their 6 — does he drop? Is he press-resistant?

Data: simple stats. Shots, possession, set pieces. Where do they score from? Where do they concede? If they concede from channel runs, that's our green light. If they score from counters, we adjust rest-defence.

In our own analysis: did we press? Did we play vertical? Was the compact block right? Three questions after every match. The game model is the benchmark.

Key: data without context is noise. Video without structure is highlights. We use both to answer specific questions: what are their weaknesses? How do we exploit them? What's our plan? The game model is the framework — data fills in the details.

prompt: What makes a good 6 in our system and how does Riedel fit?
completion:
A good 6 in our system: always available, always in position, always forward-thinking. The metronome.

Always available: when a CB is pressed, the 6 drops to offer a short option. When the 10 drifts wide, the 6 covers the pocket. When the 8 presses, the 6 holds the position. He's the outlet, the shield, the link.

Always in position: the 6 doesn't chase. If their 6 drops deep, our 8 presses — the 6 stays. If their winger cuts inside, the 6 is there to screen. He reads the game, positions before the ball arrives.

Always forward-thinking: when the 6 receives, the first look is forward. To the 10 in the pocket, or into the channel for the 9. Don't go sideways. Verticality. If the forward pass isn't on, circulate — but with intent. Build patience.

Riedel fits because he's all three. Always available — drops between CBs, offers the outlet. Always in position — shields the back four, doesn't chase. Always forward-thinking — first look is to Wendt or Hartmann. His weakness: can be pressed into errors. But that's why Schäfer covers. The 6 is the metronome — Riedel is our metronome.

prompt: How do we handle a week with three games?
completion:
Three games in a week: rotation and recovery.

Game 1 (Saturday): full strength. Best XI. Press, vertical, compact. Go for it.

Game 2 (Wednesday): rotate. Schäfer sits — he tires after 70, and three games in five days is too much. Fresh 8 starts. Heil rotates — fresh fullback if the first game was physically demanding. Loth if he ran a lot — defensive winger.

Game 3 (Saturday): back to full strength, but manage the load. If someone is carrying a knock, don't risk it. The 9 needs to sprint and press — if Hartmann is sore, don't start him. The system breaks if the 9 can't press.

Training between games: no tactical work. Recovery, light jog, stretching. The tactics are installed — three games in a week, you don't learn anything new. You execute what you know.

Key: freshness is tactical. A tired team can't press. A tired team has gaps in the compact block. Rotate smartly — don't rotate the spine (GK, CBs, 6) unless you have to. Rotate the legs (8, wingers, fullback).

Hydration, sleep, nutrition. The basics matter more than tactics when you're playing three in a week.

prompt: What's our philosophy on yellow cards and discipline?
completion:
Discipline: structure over aggression. We press hard, but we press clean.

No tactical fouls. If they beat our press, we don't pull a shirt. We recover — compact block, sprint to position. A tactical foul stops the counter, but it gives a free kick and a card. Our compact block should stop the counter without fouling.

No dissent. The referee is part of the game. Kross is the captain — he talks to the ref, nobody else. Yellow cards for dissent are wasted cards. If you get a yellow for dissent, you can't press aggressively for the rest of the game. That hurts the team.

Aggressive but fair: pressing is aggressive. Tackling is fair. Go for the ball, not the man. If you win it, transition. If you don't, compact block. No need for a foul.

If someone is on a yellow: adjust. Riedel on a yellow — Schäfer takes over pressing. Heil on a yellow — no overlapping, stay compact. Don't lose a player to a second yellow.

Key: discipline is tactical. A card changes how you can play. We need everyone pressing, everyone compact, everyone available. Cards limit that. Press hard, play clean, stay on the pitch.

prompt: How do we scout an opponent we've never seen before?
completion:
No scout: trust our principles, gather what we can.

Before the game: ask around. Other coaches, other teams. What shape do they play? Do they press or sit? Who's their danger man? Any info helps. If nothing: we prepare for everything. Our game model handles any opponent — press, vertical, compact.

First 10 minutes: read them. The 9 and wingers watch the CBs — body shape, turning speed, distribution. The 6 watches their 6 — does he drop? Is he press-resistant? The CBs watch their strikers — pace, physicality, movement.

After 10 minutes: the coach adjusts. If they press high, we beat it with verticality. If they sit deep, build patience. If their fullbacks push high, channel runs. If they counter, compact rest-defence.

Key: our game model is the scout. We don't need to know everything about them — we need to know our principles and execute them. The opponent changes the details, not the identity. Press from the front. Verticality. Compact block. Flank overload. Transition lock. That works against anyone.

After the game: we have notes. Next time, we're prepared.

prompt: What's our approach to the off-season?
completion:
Off-season: reset, reinforce, recruit.

Reset: physical recovery. The players rest — two weeks off, minimum. Then light training. No tactics, no pressing. Build the base. Fitness, strength, mobility. Come back fresh.

Reinforce: review the season. Three questions every match — did we press, did we play vertical, was the compact block right? Look at the patterns. Where did we score from? Where did we concede? The game model is the benchmark — measure everything against it.

Train the patterns more. Channel runs, flank overloads, transition lock. The connections — Wendt and Hartmann, Krüger and Heil, Riedel and Schäfer. These need to be automatic by next season.

Recruit: what do we need? A backup 8 — Schäfer tires, we need depth. A defensive winger — if Loth doesn't track back, we need an alternative. Maybe a faster CB — if Kross struggles with pace.

Pre-season: install the game model with new players. Principles first, position second. Terminology: pressing trigger, rest-defence, channel run, transition lock. If you don't understand the why, the how doesn't matter.

Key: the off-season is about coming back better, not just rested. The game model doesn't change — the execution does. Better pressing, better verticality, better compact block. Same identity, higher level.

prompt: How do we communicate on the pitch?
completion:
On-pitch communication: specific, loud, and in our terminology.

Kross is the captain — he organizes the back line. 'Compact! 18!' means tighten to 18m. 'Step!' means the line pushes up. 'Cover!' means Vogt sweeps. Short, clear, in our language.

Riedel communicates with the midfield. 'Trigger!' means the pressing trigger is on — ball-side winger collapses. 'Reset!' means drop into compact block. 'Forward!' means first look is vertical.

Hartmann communicates with the front line. 'Lane!' means he's cutting the passing lane to the CB. 'Channel!' means he's peeling into the channel. 'Press!' means first touch pressure on the GK.

Wendt communicates with the overloads. 'Drift!' means he's going wide to create the 2v1. 'Pocket!' means he's finding the space behind their CB. 'Switch!' means play to the weak side.

Key: communication is tactical, not emotional. No shouting 'come on' or 'work harder' — that's noise. 'Trigger', 'compact', 'channel', 'forward' — that's information. Each word means a specific action. The terminology isn't just for the coach — it's for the pitch. When someone says 'channel', everyone knows what's happening.

Quiet pitch = disconnected team. Loud pitch with noise = confused team. Loud pitch with terminology = organized team. That's us.

prompt: What do we do when the coach is sent off?
completion:
Coach sent off: Kross takes over on the pitch. He's the captain, he knows the game model, he reads the game.

Tactics don't change. The game model is installed — press from the front, verticality, compact block, flank overloads, transition lock. The coach doesn't need to be on the sideline for the players to execute. The principles are in their heads.

Communication: Kross organizes from the back. Riedel relays to the midfield. Hartmann relays to the front line. The structure stays — everyone has a role, everyone knows their role.

Adjustments: harder without the coach's overview. The players on the pitch have to read the game themselves. If the opponent changes shape, Kross calls the adjustment. If someone is struggling, Kross tells the bench.

Halftime: the assistant coach comes to the locker room. He knows the game model. Quick review — what's working, what's not. One or two adjustments max. Don't overhaul the plan.

Key: the game model is the coach. If the players know it, they can execute without the coach on the line. That's why we train the principles until they're automatic. The coach installs the system — the players run it. A sent-off coach is a test of whether the system is truly installed. If it is, we're fine.

prompt: How do we handle a player who doesn't follow the game model?
completion:
If a player doesn't follow the game model: talk first, bench second.

Talk: specific, not general. Not 'you're not playing well' — that's useless. 'You're going sideways when you should go forward. The 6 receives between lines, first look is to the 10 or the channel. You're playing backwards. That's not verticality.' Show them the principle, show them the moment, show them what to do differently.

If it continues: sub them. Not as punishment — as correction. 'You're not executing the plan. Sit, watch, see how it should look. Next game, you get another chance.' The bench is a teaching tool, not a punishment.

If it's a pattern: the player doesn't fit the system. That's a harder conversation. Either they adapt — learn the principles, execute them — or they don't play. The game model is non-negotiable. If you don't press, you don't play. If you don't play vertical, you don't play. If you don't stay compact, you don't play.

Key: the system is bigger than any player. We adapt the player to the system, not the system to the player. If someone can't or won't execute the game model, they're not in the team. That sounds harsh — but it's how the game model works. Everyone presses. Everyone plays vertical. Everyone stays compact. No exceptions.

prompt: What's our plan for the transfer window?
completion:
Transfer window: fill the gaps, don't change the identity.

Need 1: backup 8. Schäfer tires after 70 — we need a fresh 8 with 20 minutes to go. Someone with legs, pressing mentality, can arrive late in the box. Not a star — a worker who fits the system.

Need 2: defensive winger. Loth tracks back late. If we're holding a lead and need compact block on the left, we need a winger who defends first. Not a 1v1 specialist — a compact block player.

Need 3: maybe a faster CB. Kross is our leader but he's not the quickest. If we're playing against rapid strikers, Vogt covers. But if both CBs are slow, we're vulnerable. A young, quick CB who can learn from Kross — that's the ideal.

Don't sign: a 10 who stays central. Our 10 drifts wide — that's the flank overload. A central 10 breaks our pattern. Don't sign a 9 who doesn't press. Our 9 is the first defender — if he doesn't press, the system breaks.

Key: sign players who fit the game model, not players who are good in isolation. A great player who doesn't press is useless to us. A good player who presses, plays vertical, and stays compact is valuable. The system is the filter — talent is secondary to fit.

prompt: How do we build team chemistry around the game model?
completion:
Team chemistry: the game model is the glue. Everyone believes in the same principles, speaks the same language, executes the same plan.

Onboarding: new players learn the principles first. Not their position — the principles. Press from the front. Verticality. Compact block. Flank overload. Transition lock. If you don't understand the why, the how doesn't matter. We spend a week on this before they touch a ball in a team session.

Terminology: pressing trigger, rest-defence, channel run, switch isolation, transition lock. These aren't buzzwords — they're instructions. When Kross says 'compact', everyone knows what to do. When Riedel says 'trigger', the winger collapses. Shared language = shared understanding = chemistry.

Training: high intensity, short duration. Everyone presses, everyone runs, everyone executes. No one coasts. If someone doesn't press in training, they don't press in the game. The standard is the same.

Off-pitch: the players eat together after training. They watch matches together — opponent analysis, our own analysis. They discuss the game model. It's not just the coach's philosophy — it's the team's identity.

Key: chemistry isn't about being friends. It's about shared principles. Eleven players who all believe in pressing, verticality, and compact block — that's chemistry. Eleven players who all know what 'trigger' means and what 'channel' means — that's connection. The game model builds the team.

prompt: What's our approach to playing in extreme heat?
completion:
Extreme heat: manage the intensity, don't abandon the identity.

Pressing: shorter bursts. In heat, you can't press for 90 minutes at full intensity. Press in waves — 5 minutes of intense first touch pressure, then drop into compact block for 3 minutes to recover. Then press again. The 9 reads the energy and triggers the press.

Hydration: break in play, drink. Every stoppage — throw-in, free kick, goal kick — players drink. Kross organizes it. Don't wait until someone cramps.

Compact block: even more important in heat. If you're not pressing, you're in the block. No jogging between lines — you're either pressing or in position. Save energy by being in the right place, not by chasing.

In possession: verticality. Don't hold the ball — that expends energy. Win it, forward, two touches, shot or cross. Transition lock. The less time we have the ball, the less we run. Counter-intuitive but true — in heat, possession is exhausting.

Subs: earlier. Schäfer at 55, not 65. Fresh legs in heat are worth more than fresh legs in cool weather. Plan three subs by the 70th minute.

Key: heat changes the intensity, not the identity. We still press, still play vertical, still stay compact. But we manage the energy. Press in waves, hydrate, sub early. The game model adapts to conditions — the principles don't.

prompt: How do we use our bench effectively?
completion:
The bench is a tactical tool, not just a backup plan.

Pre-game: the bench knows the plan. If they're coming on, they know what to change. Fresh 8: press more, arrive late in the box. Defensive winger: compact block, track back. Fresh fullback: no overlapping, stay compact. Each sub has a specific role.

During the game: the coach watches who's tiring. Schäfer at 65 — the press drops before he says he's tired. Heil if he's getting caught high. Loth if he's not tracking back. Don't wait for the player to ask — the coach decides based on what he sees.

Impact subs: if we need a goal, the bench has options. A second 10 for more creativity. A big striker for crosses. A pacey winger for counters. Know what each sub brings and when to use them.

Mental: the bench stays engaged. Watch the game, analyze the opponent, be ready. A sub who comes on cold is useless. A sub who's been watching and knows what to do is a weapon.

Key: the bench wins games. Eleven players can't press for 90 minutes. The subs maintain the intensity. Fresh legs, same principles. The game model doesn't change when a sub comes on — the execution gets a boost. That's the value of the bench.

prompt: What makes FC Metall Nord different from other clubs?
completion:
Three things make us different:

1. We fine-tune our identity. The game model isn't a document that sits in a drawer — it's in the players' heads, in their movements, in their decisions. We train the principles until they're automatic. When Riedel receives between lines, he doesn't think 'forward first' — he just does it. When Hartmann sees the CB facing his own goal, he doesn't think 'pressing trigger' — he just collapses. The game model is muscle memory.

2. We play vertical. Other teams keep the ball. We go forward. Sideways is the opponent's friend — we don't do them any favors. If the 6 receives, first look is forward. If the channel is open, we play it. If the shot is on, we shoot. We don't recycle — we attack. Verticality isn't a tactic, it's a mindset.

3. We press from the front. The 9 is the first defender — that's non-negotiable. Other teams let the CB have the ball. We don't. First touch pressure on the GK, cut the lane to the CB, force mistakes. That's where our goals come from — transition lock, two touches, shot or cross.

We are FC Metall Nord. Verticality over possession. Press from the front. Compact rest-defence. Flank overloads. Transition lock. That's not a slogan — that's who we are. Every player, every minute, every game.

prompt: How do we handle a team that plays with a false 9?
completion:
False 9 means their striker drops into midfield. That opens the channel behind their midfield.

Our response: Kross steps with the false 9. If their 9 drops into our half, Kross follows him man-to-man. Vogt covers the space Kross left. Riedel screens in front of the back four.

If their 9 drops deep and we don't follow: they have a numerical advantage in midfield. So Kross steps. But Vogt must cover. If both CBs step, we're exposed.

In possession: their CBs have no one to mark. So Hartmann presses the CB anyway. First touch pressure doesn't require a target. He cuts the lane, forces the long ball. Second ball is ours.

Flank overloads: a false 9 team usually plays narrow. Their fullbacks are exposed. Kruger pins, Wendt drifts, Heil underlaps. 2v1s all day.

Key: don't get pulled into their false 9 game. Press their CBs, overload their flanks, exploit the space behind their dropping 9. Verticality. Go forward, not sideways.

prompt: What's our plan if we concede first?
completion:
Concede first: don't panic. The game model doesn't change because the score changes.

First 30 seconds after conceding: compact block. 18-20m between lines. Don't chase the ball. Let them celebrate, reset. The worst thing is to concede again because we're disorganized.

After reset: press from the front. First touch pressure. We need the ball back through structure, not desperation. Hartmann cuts the lane, wingers collapse on the CB.

In possession: verticality. Don't force long shots. Work it into the box. Channel runs, flank overloads. The same patterns that got us here will get us back.

Mental: the 9 is the first defender. If Hartmann stops pressing after conceding, the system breaks. Press from the front regardless of score.

If we're still down at 60 minutes: push Kross into the box for set pieces. Bring on fresh legs. Maybe a second striker. But the compact block stays. Don't abandon the structure to chase a goal.

Key: the game model is the game model. 0-0 or 0-1, we press, we play vertical, we stay compact. The score changes the urgency, not the identity.

prompt: How do we defend a long throw-in into the box?
completion:
Long throw into the box: treat it like a set piece. Compact marking.

Kross: marks their biggest target. Physical battle. Don't let him get a run-up. Body position, block the run.

Vogt: sweeps. Reads the flight, positions where the ball will land. If Kross contests and the ball drops, Vogt clears.

Riedel: guards the near post. Schaefer: guards the edge of the box. Clears the second ball.

Heil and Auer: mark their wide players. If the throw goes long to the back post, the fullback on that side challenges.

Brandt: commands the line. On a long throw, he can come for the ball if it's within reach. But if it's too far, stay on the line.

After clearance: sprint out. Don't let them recycle the throw. Compact block, transition readiness. If we win the second ball, transition lock: two touches, shot or cross.

Key: don't lose your man. Everyone has an assignment. Kross on the big target, Vogt sweeps, Riedel near post, Schaefer edge of the box. Simple, organized, physical.

prompt: What's our approach to the second leg of a knockout tie?
completion:
Second leg: the first leg is data. Use it.

If we won the first leg: don't sit deep. That's not us. Press from the front, same identity. But be smarter. They'll push for a goal, which opens space for our counter. Transition lock. Channel runs. They have to come at us. That's our advantage.

Compact block: 18-20m between lines. If they push players forward, the channel opens. Hartmann peels. Their CB is exposed because they've committed bodies forward.

If we lost the first leg: we need a goal. But don't abandon the structure. Verticality. Forward first. Flank overloads. Channel runs. Same patterns, more urgency.

If it's level on aggregate: it's a one-off. Press, vertical, compact. The game model decides it.

Key: the first leg tells us what worked and what didn't. Did their CB handle our channel runs? Did their press beat our build-up? Adjust, but don't overhaul. The game model is the constant. The opponent adjustments are the variables.

Set pieces: more important in knockout ties. Kross attacks the ball. Don't concede cheap fouls. A set piece can decide a knockout tie.

prompt: How do we manage a player returning from injury?
completion:
Returning from injury: ease them in. The game model doesn't change, but the load does.

First game back: start, but plan to sub at 60. Don't expect 90 minutes. The player needs to feel the game again. The pressing, the compact block, the transitions.

If it's a hamstring or muscle injury: no sprinting at 100 percent in the first 20 minutes. Let them ease into the intensity. The pressing trigger might be a step slower. That's OK. The structure covers for one player being a half-step late.

If it's a knee or long-term: start on the bench. 30 minutes. Let them see the game from the bench first. Read the opponent, understand the tempo. Then come on with fresh legs and a clear picture.

Position: if the player is the 9 (Hartmann), the pressing depends on him. If he can't press at 100 percent, the system is compromised. Consider starting him on the bench and bringing him on for the last 30 when the opponent is tired and he can press in bursts.

Training: no full-contact in the first week back. Light tactical work. Positioning, movements, patterns. The game model is installed. The returning player knows it. He just needs to get his body back.

Key: the player's health is more important than one game. A re-injury costs weeks. Ease them in, manage the load, trust the structure to cover.

prompt: What do we do if the referee is calling everything tight?
completion:
Tight referee: adjust the pressing intensity, not the structure.

If every contact is a foul: don't go to ground. Press with positioning, not contact. Body shape. Block the passing lane, don't tackle. Force the error, don't create the foul.

First touch pressure: Hartmann cuts the lane to the CB. That's positioning, not a tackle. Legal. The CB has to play long because Hartmann is in the lane, not because Hartmann fouled him.

Compact block: 18-20m between lines. If the ref is tight, give an extra meter. 20-22m. Less contact, same structure. The compact block doesn't require contact. It requires positioning.

Transition: if we win it, two touches, shot or cross. No fouls in transition. That's where cards happen. Play clean, play fast.

Kross: as captain, talk to the ref early. Not arguing. Clarifying. What's the line today? If the ref tells you, adjust. If not, read the first few calls and adapt.

Key: don't change the game model. Change the intensity of contact. Press with positioning, not tackles. Compact with space, not grabbing. The structure works without fouls. Discipline over aggression.

prompt: How do we prepare for a match on artificial turf?
completion:
Artificial turf: the ball behaves differently. Faster, lower bounce, more predictable roll.

Passing: shorter, crisper. The ball doesn't slow down on turf. It runs faster. First touch is critical. Control it dead, don't let it skip away. Riedel and Schaefer work on first touch in warm-up.

Pressing: the ball runs faster, so the pressing trigger fires earlier. The CB receives and the ball is at his feet instantly. The winger has less time to collapse. Start the press one step earlier. Anticipate, don't react.

Channel runs: the ball runs through faster on turf. Hartmann's channel run. The ball arrives quicker, which is good. But the CB also recovers faster because the ball doesn't slow. Play the ball earlier. Wendt finds Hartmann before the run, not during.

Set pieces: the ball rolls faster on the ground. Short corners work better. The ball reaches the near post quicker. Kross attacks the ball. On turf, the delivery is flatter. Adjust the crossing angle.

Sliding: don't slide on turf. Burns. Auer and Heil. No sliding tackles. Position, block, force the pass. If you can't win it without sliding, don't try. Compact block and wait.

Key: the game model doesn't change. The ball behaves differently, so the execution adjusts. Crisper passing, earlier pressing, earlier channel balls, no sliding. Same identity, turf-adjusted execution.

prompt: What's our strategy for a midweek game after a weekend match?
completion:
Midweek after weekend: recovery is the tactic. You can't press for 90 if you played 90 three days ago.

Rotation: Schaefer sits. He's the box-to-box, he runs the most. Fresh 8 starts. Heil rotates if the weekend was physical. Fresh fullback. Loth if he ran a lot. Defensive winger.

Don't rotate the spine: Brandt, Kross, Vogt, Riedel, Hartmann. These five are the structure. If they're fit, they play. The legs around them rotate.

Pressing: shorter bursts. In a midweek game, you can't press for 90 at full intensity. Press in waves. 5 minutes intense, 3 minutes compact block. The 9 reads the energy and triggers.

Training between games: no tactical work. Recovery, light jog, stretching. The tactics are installed. Three days, you don't learn anything new. Execute what you know.

Hydration and sleep: the basics matter more than tactics. Eight hours sleep, hydration, good food. A tired team can't press. Freshness is tactical.

In possession: verticality. Don't hold the ball. That expends energy. Win it, forward, two touches, shot or cross. Transition lock. The less time we have the ball, the less we run.

Key: the game model stays, the load management changes. Rotate the legs, keep the spine. Press in waves, not for 90. Recovery is the tactic.

prompt: How do we handle a team that plays with three at the back and wingbacks?
completion:
Three at the back: the wingbacks are the key. They provide width. If we pin them, their shape collapses.

Flank overload: their wingback gets forward but has to track back. Kruger pins him, Wendt drifts, Heil underlaps. If the wingback doesn't track back, 2v1 all day. If he does, he's tired by 60.

Channel runs: 3-at-the-back means three CBs. The channel between the wide CB and the wingback is the target. Hartmann peels into that channel. The wide CB has to choose: follow Hartmann or hold the line. Either way, we exploit it.

Build patience: 3-at-the-back teams often sit in a mid-block. Don't force it. Circulate, wait for their middle CB to step. When he steps, Wendt finds the pocket behind him.

Rest-defence: their wingbacks push high, which means their back three is spread. If we win it, the channel is wide open. Transition lock. Two touches, exploit the space.

Against the ball: their wingbacks provide width in possession. If we press the wingback, ball-side winger collapses, they have no width. They're forced inside, into our compact block. 18-20m between lines.

Key: 3-at-the-back is about the wingbacks. Pin them, exploit the channels, build patience against the mid-block. Same game model, flank-focused execution.

prompt: What's our approach to a friendly match?
completion:
Friendly: same game model, different stakes. Use it to test and refine.

Starting XI: best team. The friendly is a dress rehearsal. Treat it like a real match. Press from the front, verticality, compact block. The game model doesn't have a friendly mode.

Substitutions: more than a competitive match. Sub at 30, 45, 60, 75. Give everyone minutes. But each sub has a purpose. Testing a new 8, giving a returning player 30 minutes, trying Loth in a different role.

Experiment: within the game model. Don't try a back three. That's not us. But try Wendt in a deeper role. Try Kruger as an inverted winger. Try Schaefer as the 6. Small experiments within the structure.

Evaluation: three questions after the friendly. Did we press? Did we play vertical? Was the compact block right? The game model is the benchmark, even in a friendly.

Data: use the friendly to collect. Player ratings. How did each player execute the principles? Opponent analysis. What did they do that surprised us? Feed it back into training.

Key: a friendly is not a break from the game model. It's a test of it. Same identity, more experimentation, more evaluation. The game model gets stronger with every match.

prompt: How do we use video analysis to improve our pressing?
completion:
Video analysis for pressing: watch the triggers, not the highlights.

What to watch: the moment their CB receives the ball. Does our winger collapse? Does the 9 cut the lane? How fast does the trigger fire? If the winger is a step late, that's the fix. The pressing trigger needs to be faster.

What to count: how many times did we press the GK? How many times did the first touch pressure force a long ball? How many second balls did we win? These are the metrics of our pressing, not possession, not shots.

What to look for: patterns. If their CB always plays to the same side, our winger should anticipate. If their GK always goes long to the left channel, Riedel positions there. Video reveals habits. We exploit them.

What to show the team: 5-10 clips, not 60. The best pressing trigger. This is what it looks like. The worst. This is what happens when the trigger is late. Two clips per principle. Short, clear, actionable.

What to train next: the answer comes from the video. If the winger is late on the trigger, train the trigger. If the 9 doesn't cut the lane, train the lane cut. Video tells you what to fix. Training fixes it.

Key: video is a tool, not a movie. Watch the triggers, count the pressures, find the patterns. Five clips, two principles, one training session. That's how video improves pressing.

prompt: What's our plan for the last 10 minutes when we're drawing?
completion:
Last 10 minutes, drawing: go for it, but with structure. Don't abandon the game model.

Push Kross into the box for set pieces. Schaefer makes late runs. If we have a corner, Brandt comes up. But the 6 stays. Riedel holds the shield. Don't empty the midfield.

In possession: crosses into the box. Hartmann near post, Kross attacking the ball, Schaefer arriving late. Channel runs still work. If their CB steps to deal with the cross, Hartmann peels into the channel.

Flank overloads: both fullbacks can overlap now. We need numbers. But one stays compact. Heil overlaps on the right, Auer holds on the left. Don't both go. If we lose it, we're exposed.

Transition: if we win a corner, take it quickly. Don't waste time. Two touches in transition, shot or cross. Same rule. Transition lock doesn't expire in the last 10.

Mental: don't panic. A draw is not a loss. But if the chance is there, take it. Verticality. Forward first. Don't recycle in the last 10. If the shot is on, shoot. If the cross is on, cross.

Subs: fresh legs. A pacey winger for counters. A big striker for crosses. But each sub fits the game model. They press, they play vertical, they stay compact.

Key: go for it with structure. More bodies forward, but the 6 stays. Crosses, channel runs, set pieces. Same identity, more urgency. The game model doesn't have a panic mode. It has a push mode. Push with structure.

prompt: How do we handle playing at high altitude?
completion:
High altitude: the air is thin. You can't press for 90. Your lungs won't allow it.

Pressing: shorter bursts, longer recovery. 3 minutes of intense first touch pressure, then 5 minutes of compact block. The 9 reads the energy. If the team is gassed, drop into the block. Don't press on empty lungs.

Hydration: critical at altitude. More water, more electrolytes. Break in play, drink. Every stoppage. Kross organizes it.

Subs: earlier and more. Schaefer at 50, not 65. The box-to-box engine runs out of air faster at altitude. Fresh 8 with 40 minutes. Maybe three subs by the 70th.

In possession: verticality. Don't hold the ball. At altitude, possession is exhausting. Win it, forward, two touches, shot or cross. Transition lock. The less time we have the ball, the less we run.

Compact block: even more important. 18-20m between lines, maybe tighter. 15m. Less chasing, more positioning. If you're in the right place, you don't need to sprint.

Rest-defence: no overlapping. Fullbacks stay compact. At altitude, recovery runs are brutal. Heil and Auer hold position. No chasing shadows.

Key: altitude changes the intensity, not the identity. Press in shorter bursts, hydrate, sub earlier, play more vertical. The game model adapts to conditions. The principles don't.

prompt: How do we handle a team that marks man-to-man across the field?
completion:
Man-to-man marking: exploit the space they leave. If everyone has a man, no one is covering space.

Movement: drag your man out of position. Wendt drifts wide, his marker follows. That opens the pocket for Schaefer to arrive late. Hartmann peels into the channel, his marker follows. That opens the pocket for Wendt.

Overloads: if their marker follows our winger, the 10 drifts wide to create a 2v1 against one marker. The marker can't mark both. If they switch markers, we exploit the confusion.

Build-up: if they mark man-to-man, our CBs have their strikers marking them. Kross and Vogt carry the ball. If their striker follows, they're out of position. Drive forward, draw the marker, release to Riedel who is free.

Transition: man-to-man is vulnerable to transitions. When we win it, their markers are spread. Each following their man. The space is between the markers. Two touches, shot or cross. Transition lock.

Rest-defence: if their striker marks our CB, our CB has space to carry the ball. But if we lose it, their striker is already in our half. Danger. Compact block immediately.

Key: man-to-man is about movement. Drag markers, create overloads, exploit the space between markers. The game model works. Verticality, flank overloads, channel runs. Man-to-man just makes the space more predictable.

prompt: How do we prepare for a match that's televised?
completion:
Televised match: same game model, more attention. Don't change anything tactical.

The camera adds pressure, but the pitch is the same size. The ball is the same. The opponent is the same. Press from the front, verticality, compact block. The game model doesn't know it's on TV.

Kick-off time: if it's moved for TV, adjust the schedule. Meal times, warm-up times, travel. The routine matters. Don't let TV disrupt the preparation. Same warm-up, same timeline, just shifted.

Mental: some players get nervous on camera. Kross talks to them. Same game, same principles. The camera doesn't tackle you. The game model is the comfort. When in doubt, press. When in doubt, play forward. When in doubt, compact block.

Tactical: no changes. The temptation is to play safe on TV. Don't. Safe means sideways, and sideways is not verticality. Safe means deep, and deep is not pressing from the front. We are FC Metall Nord. We press, we play vertical, we stay compact. On TV or not.

Media: the coach handles media. Players focus on the game. No interviews before the match. Focus. After the match, yes. Before, no.

Key: the game model is the routine. TV changes the schedule, not the tactics. Same warm-up, same principles, same identity. The camera is a spectator. We play for the result, not the broadcast.

prompt: What do we do if our 6 gets injured during the match?
completion:
Riedel injured mid-match: the 6 is the metronome. Losing him is losing the heartbeat. But the structure can adapt.

Immediate: Schaefer drops to the 6. He's the 8, box-to-box, pressing engine. He can play 6. Less forward runs, more screening. The 8 position is filled by a sub. Fresh 8 comes on.

If no sub available: Wendt drops deeper. The 10 becomes a second 6. We go 4-2-3-1 with two 6s. Less creativity, more stability. Compact block. 18-20m between lines. The structure holds.

If Riedel can walk but not run: keep him on as a positional 6. He stands in the right place, directs traffic, doesn't chase. Schaefer does the running for both. It's not ideal, but the metronome's positioning is as important as his running.

Tactical adjustment: if the 6 is compromised, the pressing drops. The 9 still presses, but the midfield press is weaker. Compact block becomes more important. 15m between lines. Let them have it deeper, press the trigger in our half instead of theirs.

In possession: without Riedel's metronome, the build-up is less structured. Kross and Vogt carry more. The 8 drops to receive. Verticality. Don't try to replace Riedel's passing, just go forward faster.

Key: the 6 is the most important position in our system. Losing him hurts. But the structure adapts. Schaefer drops, compact block tightens, verticality compensates. The game model survives injuries because it's a structure, not an individual.

prompt: How do we use the bench to change the game in the second half?
completion:
Second-half subs: change the game, not the game model. Each sub has a specific purpose.

If we need a goal: fresh 8 for Schaefer at 65. More energy in the press, late runs into the box. Second striker for Loth at 70. Go 4-4-2, two channel runners, more pressure on their CBs. Pacey winger for Kruger at 75. If their LB is tired, fresh pace exposes him.

If we're holding a lead: defensive winger for Loth at 60. Compact block, tracks back, 1v1 only. Fresh CB for Kross at 75. If Kross is booked or tired, fresh CB secures the back. Fresh 6 for Riedel at 80. If Riedel is on a yellow or tired, fresh 6 holds the shield.

If the opponent changed shape: if they go 3-5-2, bring on a second striker to exploit the channels. If they go 4-4-2 deep, bring on a creative 10 to break the block. If they go all-out attack, bring on a defensive 6, go 4-5-1, compact block.

Timing: sub at 60-65, not 80. A sub at 80 has 10 minutes. A sub at 65 has 25. Earlier subs win games.
